ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Twice Cooked Pork (Hui Guo Rou). Closest defensible anchor is pork belly food product (FOODON_03311737). The prepared dish includes additional ingredients (doubanjiang, douchi, garlic leeks) not captured by this term.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LN (Pork, cooked) is the closest defensible match. No specific FoodEx2 entry exists for Twice Cooked Pork as a composite Sichuan dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant or home serving of Twice Cooked Pork is approximately 250 g, typically served alongside a bowl of steamed white rice.

nutrient_source.title: Pork, fresh, belly (side), raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 168323); adapted for cooked twice-cooked preparation with fermented condiments
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Twice Cooked Pork exists in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy pork belly (FDC ID 168323) combined with USDA entries for fermented soybean condiments and cooked garlic leeks, adjusted for cooking losses and sauce absorption consistent with published Sichuan dish composition studies. Core macros (energy 285 kcal, protein 12.5 g, fat 24.5 g, carbohydrate 4.5 g) are consistent with this adaptation. Sodium (520 mg/100 g) reflects the high fermented-condiment load typical of this dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — analogous estimate based on mixed pork and vegetable stir-fry with fermented soy condiments
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC — Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Twice Cooked Pork. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on the analogous category of mixed meat-and-vegetable stir-fry dishes with fermented soy sauces, which are predominantly fat- and protein-dominant with minimal digestible carbohydrate (4.5 g/100 g). Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (35 × 11.25) / 100 ≈ 3.9. This is consistent with the low-GI, low-GL profile of high-fat, high-protein Sichuan pork dishes.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (520 mg/100 g; ~1300 mg per 250 g serving) from doubanjiang, douchi, and sweet bean sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Twice Cooked Pork due to its very high sodium load from fermented bean pastes; if consumed, portion size should be restricted to no more than 100 g and paired with low-sodium foods throughout the day.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (4.5 g/100 g) and low GI (estimated 35) are favorable for glycemic control, but high saturated fat (8.9 g/100 g) and caloric density (285 kcal/100 g) may impair insulin sensitivity with regular consumption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Twice Cooked Pork has a low glycemic index and minimal carbohydrate load, making it suitable for short-term blood glucose management in type 2 diabetes; however, its high saturated fat and caloric density warrant portion control (≤150 g) and infrequent consumption to avoid long-term metabolic risk.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (8.9 g/100 g), high sodium (520 mg/100 g), and high caloric density from pork belly contribute to atherogenic and hypertensive risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should treat Twice Cooked Pork as an occasional food only, limiting servings to no more than 100 g and choosing preparations with reduced sauce to lower saturated fat and sodium intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat content may promote pro-inflammatory pathways; spicy components (Sichuan peppercorn, doubanjiang) may exacerbate symptoms in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should consume Twice Cooked Pork in moderation; the ginger and garlic provide modest anti-inflammatory benefit, but the overall high saturated fat profile may worsen systemic inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Twice Cooked Pork (回锅肉, Huíguōròu) is one of the most iconic dishes of Sichuan (Szechuan) cuisine, originating in Sichuan Province, China. The dish is defined by its two-stage cooking process: pork belly is first simmered in water with aromatics — ginger, scallions, and Sichuan peppercorns — until nearly tender, then removed, cooled, and sliced thin. In the second stage, the sliced pork is stir-fried at high heat in a wok until the fat renders and the edges curl into the characteristic 'lamp-wick' shape. The pork is then seasoned with a bold sauce combining doubanjiang (spicy fermented broad-bean paste), douchi (fermented black beans), and sweet bean sauce (tianmianjiang), creating a deeply savory, spicy, and slightly sweet flavor profile. Green garlic leeks or flat cabbage are stir-fried alongside the pork, providing a fresh, crunchy contrast. Nutritionally, Twice Cooked Pork is energy-dense at approximately 285 kcal per 100 g, with high fat (24.5 g, of which ~8.9 g is saturated), moderate protein (12.5 g), and low carbohydrate (4.5 g). Sodium content is very high at 520 mg/100 g due to the fermented condiments. The dish has a low estimated glycemic index (~35) and minimal glycemic load, making it suitable for blood glucose management in isolation, but its high saturated fat and sodium content make it a concern for cardiovascular and hypertensive patients. It is a staple of Sichuan home cooking and restaurant menus, typically served with steamed white rice at lunch or dinner.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Twice Cooked Pork as a prepared composite dish exists in USDA FoodData Central, Singapore HPB, or other approved primary sources. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy pork belly data combined with fermented condiment and vegetable components. Core macros are internally consistent and plausible for this dish type. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: water_g set to -1: moisture content for the finished composite dish (pork belly + fermented condiments + stir-fried vegetables) is not directly reported in USDA FoodData Central or any other approved source consulted as of 2026-04-16; no approved source provides a moisture value for this specific prepared Sichuan dish. Saturated fat (8.9 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (10.8 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (2.6 g), trans_fat_g (0.1 g), and cholesterol_mg (65 mg) are estimated from USDA SR Legacy pork belly fatty acid profile (FDC ID 168323) adjusted for cooking method; these are not directly measured for the composite dish. The input source (Sichuan University of Science & Engineering, https://www.suse.edu.cn) is not on the approved source list and has therefore been replaced with USDA FoodData Central as the primary nutrient reference. The duplicate portion entry in the input (two identical '1 serving (250g)' entries) has been deduplicated to a single 250 g serving. GI value of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Twice Cooked Pork was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (35 × 4.5 g carbohydrate × 2.5) / 100 ≈ 3.9 g. This dish is not Singaporean; G6 multilingual requirements for Singaporean dishes (Malay, Tamil, Hindi) do not apply. Chinese-script names are provided as the dish is of Chinese (Sichuan) origin.

q1_food_identity.notes: food_name is 'Twice Cooked Pork', a precise canonical English name. Chinese simplified (回锅肉) and traditional (回鍋肉) names are present. Cuisine is Szechuan/Sichuan. G6 multilingual requirement satisfied for Chinese-origin dish.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal (285.0), protein_g (12.5), fat_g (24.5), carbohydrate_g (4.5) all present and numeric. nutrient_source references USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (approved source) with adaptation documented. No zero macros requiring justification.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (0.8), sodium_mg (520.0), saturated_fat_g (8.9), sugar_g (1.2) all present as numeric values. Values are plausible for a pork belly stir-fry with fermented condiments.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index (35), gi_category ('low'), glycemic_load_per_serving (3.9 per 250 g serving), and gi_source all present. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous category with estimation basis documented. Carbohydrate content (4.5 g/100 g) is not negligible, so GI is applicable (not null).

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn anchor FOODON_03311737 (pork belly food product) is defensible with gap note and match_type 'closest_parent'. FoodEx2 A00LN (Pork, cooked) is defensible with gap note and match_type 'closest_parent'. SNOMED CT is null with gap note. No codes invented.

Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match_type values from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent'; added water_g = -1 justification to notes field.

Iteration 3: Expanded water_g = -1 documentation in notes to explicitly state the reason data is unavailable and confirm no approved source provides moisture data for this specific prepared dish, resolving g3_numeric_hygiene gate failure from validator feedback.
